questions.stem: A plain reference such as A1 that changes when you copy the formula to another cell is called what type of reference?
questions.explanation: A relative reference adjusts itself based on where the formula is copied, so A1 becomes A2 when the formula moves down a row. An absolute reference is locked and never shifts, a mixed reference locks only part, and a circular reference is an error where a cell refers back to itself.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: A reference that is locked so it always points to the very same cell, even after copying, is called what?
questions.explanation: An absolute reference stays fixed on one cell no matter where the formula is copied. In Excel it is written by placing dollar signs before the column letter and the row number. A relative reference, by contrast, shifts to match the new position.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: Which symbol is placed in front of a column letter or row number to lock that part of a cell reference in Excel?
questions.explanation: The dollar sign is the locking symbol. Putting it before the column letter freezes the column, and putting it before the row number freezes the row. Locking both makes the reference fully absolute. The other symbols have unrelated meanings in Excel.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: The formula =A1 is in cell B1. If you copy it down into cell B2, what does the copied formula become?
questions.explanation: Because A1 is a relative reference, copying the formula down by one row pushes the reference down by one row too, so it becomes =A2. Relative references move by the same amount and direction as the formula is copied.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: The formula =A1 is in cell B1. If you copy it one cell to the right into C1, what does the copied formula become?
questions.explanation: A1 is relative, so copying the formula one column to the right shifts the reference one column to the right as well, turning A1 into B1. The row number is unchanged because the copy did not move up or down.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: The formula =A1+B1 is in cell C1. If you copy it down to C3, what does the copied formula become?
questions.explanation: Copying down by two rows (from row 1 to row 3) shifts both relative references down by two rows, so A1 becomes A3 and B1 becomes B3, giving =A3+B3. Each relative reference moves independently but by the same offset.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: A formula contains an absolute (fully locked) reference to cell A1. If you copy that formula to a completely different cell, which cell does the locked reference still point to?
options: The cell directly above the copy
questions.explanation: An absolute reference is locked, so it keeps pointing at A1 no matter where the formula is copied. That is the whole purpose of making a reference absolute: it stops the reference from shifting when the formula is reused elsewhere.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: You want a formula to always multiply each value by the tax rate stored in cell E1, so that E1 never shifts when the formula is copied down a column. What kind of reference should you use for E1?
options: A reference to the cell below E1
No reference, just type the number once
questions.explanation: If E1 were relative, copying the formula down would move it to E2, E3 and so on, away from the rate. Making the reference to E1 absolute locks it in place so every copied row still reads the single rate cell. This is the classic reason to use an absolute reference.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: A reference where only part is locked, for example the column is fixed but the row can change, is called what?
questions.explanation: A mixed reference locks one part and leaves the other free. Locking the column but not the row, or the row but not the column, both count as mixed. A fully relative reference locks nothing, and a fully absolute reference locks both parts.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: In a mixed reference the column letter is locked but the row number is left relative. If the formula is copied DOWN several rows, what happens to that reference?
options: Both the column and row change
The column changes and the row stays the same
The column stays the same and the row number changes
Both the column and row stay the same
questions.explanation: Copying down moves the row part of a reference, but here only the column is locked. So the locked column letter is unchanged while the unlocked row number increases as you move down. The locked part resists the copy; the relative part follows it.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: In a mixed reference the row number is locked but the column letter is left relative. If the formula is copied to the RIGHT across several columns, what happens to that reference?
options: The row stays the same and the column letter changes
Both the row and column change
Both the row and column stay the same
The row changes and the column stays the same
questions.explanation: Copying to the right moves the column part of a reference. Since only the row is locked here, the locked row number stays fixed while the unlocked column letter advances as you move across. The locked row holds still; the relative column shifts.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: The formula =SUM(A1:A3) is in cell B1. If you copy it down to B2, what does the copied formula become?
questions.explanation: Both ends of the range are relative, so copying down one row shifts each by one row: A1 becomes A2 and A3 becomes A4, giving =SUM(A2:A4). The whole range slides down together to keep the same shape relative to the formula.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: Which function key in Excel cycles a reference through absolute, mixed and relative forms while you are editing a formula?
questions.explanation: Pressing F4 while the cursor is on a reference adds and removes the locking dollar signs, cycling through fully locked, locked row, locked column, and fully relative. F1 opens Help, F12 opens Save As, and F2 switches a cell into edit mode.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: The formula =A1*2 is in cell B1. If you copy it down to B4, what does the copied formula become?
questions.explanation: The reference A1 is relative and the number 2 is a fixed constant. Copying down three rows shifts A1 to A4 but leaves the constant 2 unchanged, giving =A4*2. Constants typed directly into a formula never adjust when copied.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: If you copy a formula but want a particular cell reference inside it to NOT change at all, you should make that reference what?
options: Mixed with the column locked only
Mixed with the row locked only
questions.explanation: To stop a reference changing in any direction, lock both the column and the row, which makes it fully absolute. A relative reference would change in both directions, and either mixed form would still change in one direction, so only the fully absolute form is guaranteed not to move.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: The formula =B2+C2 is in cell D2. If you copy it down to D5, what does the copied formula become?
questions.explanation: Both references are relative, so moving down three rows turns B2 into B5 and C2 into C5, giving =B5+C5. This is the everyday behaviour that lets you write one row formula and fill it down a whole column.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: Why is making relative references the default behaviour so useful when building a column of totals?
options: It prevents Excel from ever recalculating
It converts every number into text
It stops the formula from referring to any cells
One formula can be copied down and it automatically adjusts to each row
questions.explanation: Relative references mean you can write the formula once for the first row, then fill it down, and each copy points at its own row. This saves rewriting the formula for every line and is the main reason relative referencing is the default.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: The formula =A1 is in cell A2. If you copy it up into cell A1 (one row up), what does the copied formula reference become, assuming no error checking blocks it?
options: It shifts up one row, to the row above A1
questions.explanation: Relative references move in the same direction as the copy. Copying one row up shifts the reference one row up from A1, that is to the row above row 1. Because row 1 is the top, this would actually point off the sheet, which is why such an upward copy can produce a reference error. The key idea is that relative references move up when the formula is copied up.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: A reference that accidentally refers back to the cell that contains the formula, creating a loop, is known as what?
questions.explanation: A circular reference happens when a formula depends, directly or through a chain, on its own cell, so Excel cannot settle on a value and warns you. It is unrelated to absolute or relative referencing, which are about how references shift when copied.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: The formula =A1*B1 is in cell C1, with B1 locked as an absolute reference and A1 left relative. If you copy the formula down to C2, what does it become?
questions.explanation: A1 is relative, so it shifts down to A2, but B1 is locked, so it stays as B1. The copied formula multiplies the new row value in A2 by the single fixed cell B1. Only the unlocked reference follows the copy.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: You build a multiplication table and want every cell to read its row label from column A and its column label from row 1. The reference to the column-A label should keep its column fixed but let its row vary, and the reference to the row-1 label should keep its row fixed but let its column vary. What kind of references are these two?
questions.explanation: Each reference locks one part and frees the other, which is the definition of a mixed reference. Locking the column while freeing the row, and locking the row while freeing the column, are the two mixed forms, and using them is the standard trick for building a multiplication grid with one copied formula.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: The formula =A1 is in cell B1. If you copy it to cell D3 (two columns right and two rows down), what does the copied reference become?
questions.explanation: A relative reference moves by the same offset as the copy. Moving two columns right turns A into C, and moving two rows down turns row 1 into row 3, giving =C3. Both the column and row parts shift because the copy moved in both directions.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: Which of these correctly describes a fully relative reference compared with a fully absolute reference when a formula is copied?
options: A relative reference adjusts to the new location while an absolute reference stays fixed
A relative reference stays fixed while an absolute reference adjusts
questions.explanation: Relative references move with the copy to match the new position, while absolute references are locked and do not move at all. The two behave in opposite ways on copying, which is precisely why Excel offers both kinds.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible

questions.stem: The formula =SUM(B2:B4) is in cell B5 and you copy it one column to the right into C5. What does the copied formula become?
questions.explanation: Both ends of the range are relative, so copying one column to the right shifts the column letter from B to C while the row numbers stay the same, giving =SUM(C2:C4). This is how a total written under one column can be filled across to total the neighbouring columns.
questions.source: in the style of the Excel 2019 Bible
